113 - What Are The Pros and Cons Of Getting Into The Sales Industry- - Sales Training - YouTube.mp4
So what are the pros and cons of you getting into sales, a career in sales? Let's say you're looking to become a salesperson. What do you need to know that's really the pros of the industry and what are the cons? So if you get in, you know how to avoid the cons of the industry because why struggle, why fail when you don't have to when you're taught the right skills, right? First of all, go down to the bottom of this video, hit the subscribe button. That's probably important for you. Hit the subscribe button and to the right of that, or maybe the left, I don't know. Somewhere in there, there's like a little bell. That's your notifications button.
Hit the notifications button as well so you get notified by YouTube every time I post a new training video, which I typically do two to four times a week. So the biggest pro is commission-based, okay? That means you just don't get capped out. Like if you sell a ton, you make a ton of money that a salary position is just not going to give you. Like if you get a job in admin, operations, or finance, you're gonna be capped at a certain level no matter how well you do at your job. With sales, if you outsell everybody else, I guarantee you the ownership of your company is gonna reward you more than anybody else because they're never gonna wanna lose you. So, you're always gonna get paid the most, okay?
You can always make the most money as a commission-based salesperson. In fact, when I was in sales and companies tried to like pay me a big base and a lower commission, I would always negotiate them down on the base, pay me way lower of a base. Like some companies I said don't even pay me a base at all, pay me all commissions, and they're like sure, okay, and then I just took off and I could make 10 times more money because I didn't have any ceiling. But you have to be, at that point, you have to really understand the sales process. You have to be confident in your sales ability because you've acquired skills. Once you've got that, why would you wanna get a pay?
Because you know how to sell, you just want commission only, right? There's a lot more flexibility, especially if you perform. Now, if you don't perform, not so much flexibility, but if you perform, you can travel. Sometimes companies will let you work from home because, look, they don't wanna lose you So this was even pre-COVID, back in my sales career. I remember back in like 2006, 2000, 2008, I got my second sales career. I was in B2B sales in my second job; my first was B2C. But when I got into B2B sales, within like three months, I was the number one salesperson in this entire company. They had about 400 salespeople nationwide. I became the number one like overnight. I was making five times more sales than anybody else in the company, and they had a lot of big hitters.
So I was like, you know what, I think I wanna start working from home. Are you guys cool with that if I mainly work from home? What are they gonna say? Yeah, so they had me come in the office like one day a week, sometimes two. They gave me the biggest corner office, and I was only there not even half the time. I had a bigger office than the CEO of the freaking company. Corner suite, it was like this huge, overlooking everything, and they did that because they didn't want to lose me, because I was selling probably 40 plus million dollars worth of revenue for them every single year. Just one freaking rep, I was making like one to 1. 8 million dollars a year in commissions from that job.
Okay, eventually I made over two million a year in that job So they gave you a lot of flexibility, so if you sell well, you perform well, because you've acquired the right skills, they'll let you travel, as long as you can make your calls and appointments from your laptop. They'll let you work from home, they'll let you do anything, as long as you perform. I can pretty much assure you that. Now, here's what you wanna do though, you want to learn how to sell. Now, if you're looking at getting into a career in sales, we hear this all the time, oh, yeah, I mean, I don't even have a sales job, like I need to get my sales job, then I'm gonna acquire the skills how to sell.
I'm like, that's like a neurosurgeon saying that he's gonna get a job as a neurosurgeon, and then once he starts operating on the brain, he's gonna learn how to operate on the brain. I don't know about that, I don't think I'd wanna hire that brain surgeon, right? And employers don't want to hire salespeople that have no skills, that are completely raw, that are not certified in courses. In fact, one of the biggest questions we have employers ask that we represent, that we train their salespeople when they're interviewing somebody new, is how much money did you personally invest in learning advanced skills so you could sell more to try to get this job And if they're like, well, I bought a book, or the company before brought in a guy to teach us some sales on a weekend, they're not really that serious about their career because they're not investing in their skill level, like people that have high-level professions do.
But if they're like, hey, I bought, usually I bought one or two books a month, I've gone to three or four seminars this year, I bought a few courses for two or three or four grand, that's the person they're gonna hire because they know they're serious about their profession. So if you want to get into sales, when's the best time to learn how to sell? Before you get the sales job. So day number one, when you pick up the phone and the prospect says hello, you actually know how to sell and close the deals. Because if you can't, if you're trying to learn on the job, guess what happens after two, three, four weeks when you're hardly selling anything? You're gonna get fired and canned, and it's gonna be hard for you to ever get back into sales.
You have to learn how to sell before you get the job, so when you start day number one, you actually are successful from day number one. Crazy, all right. Now, the cool thing about the pros of the sales career is you get to help people solve their problems. You know, like if you've ever been like a detective, like you always like asking questions, you always like helping people solve problems, sales is the perfect position for you, okay? Because you wanna help them find problems they didn't even know they had. That's called problem finding and once you find the problems when they purchase from you, then you can solve the problems and I can assure you, there's no greater feeling than your prospects giving testimonials about how you help them do this and do that and remove stress from their life and frustration by solving their problems and getting them where they want to go.
In fact, it's gonna be so good for you that your office, the peers, they admire you. It's such a, it's a really good feeling, right? You get awards, you get trophies, you get trips. If you acquire the right skills, it is the best profession you can do to get rewarded, okay? So you want that. Now, top achievements, you're always recognized. Most companies are gonna recognize their top salespeople, they just will. You perform well, you acquire the right skills, you're always gonna be rewarded. Like I said, you're gonna go on trips, you're gonna get cash bonuses, you're gonna get year-end bonuses, like it is a big profession that will pay you a lot once you acquire the right skills. And the cool thing is, like I said, your prospects will love you if you learn how to sell the right way.
Now, if you're some pushy, crazy, manipulative salesperson, they're not gonna like you and you're not gonna sell that well because that's what average salespeople do in our day and age. Like top 1% earning salespeople who make hundreds of thousands of dollars a year or more, their prospects love them. In fact, they're so good and we can teach you these skills. They're so good that their prospects don't even feel like they're on a sales call with them. They quite literally are thanking the salesperson and allowing them to give the salesperson money to solve their problems and get where they want to be, and that's a really good feeling to have. I can assure you of that. Now, those are the pros. There's a lot of other pros. I probably forgot, but here you go.
Here are the cons, all right? Let me, I'm gonna push the cons over here for you, okay? The cons are less money if you don't acquire the right skills. Sales can be a very low profession, low-paying profession if you don't acquire the right skills to be able to sell more. If you're using pushy, traditional selling skills from the 80s or 90s, or repackaged as consultative selling, where your prospects start slapping you around, get to the point, enough with the questions, and they just go into fight or flight mode and try to get rid of you, it can suck if you don't have the right skills. I mean, it's like, if you have the right skills, you can make a ton of money. You have the wrong skills, you ain't gonna make any money.
It's just like either you're up here or you're down here, all right? Now, here's what you wanna be careful of. If companies give you a big base but a low commission, like pharmaceutical companies are notorious for this. They'll give you a base of like 100 grand a year, and even if you sell everything, every doctor you talk to, they're only gonna pay you 30 or 40 grand more for commissions. You end up making 130 or 140. It's because your freaking base is so big. They give that base to everybody, and no matter how much they sell, they just can't get up to 250, 350, 500, 800, a million dollars a year in commissions a lot of times in that industry because they don't know how to negotiate a high commission level and a very low base.
So you want to be careful of companies like that. You want to always negotiate a high commission base, all right? Now, another thing that is a con if you don't learn how to do it, if you don't acquire the right skills, you're going to get loads of rejection and objections. Now, I'm assuming you don't like getting rejected You don't like getting a lot of objections, so you acquire the right skills; you really take that out of the equation. So that's a very must. The con is, if you don't acquire the right skills, you're gonna get punched in the nose, I can assure you, okay? You don't have any flexibility if you don't perform. You perform, you have flexibility. That's a pro.
If you can't perform, because it all comes down to you acquiring the right skills, really, if you can't perform, well, they're not gonna let you travel around; they're not gonna let you work from home. They're gonna put you in an office in a cubicle and hear your calls and watch you all day like a freaking vulture until they just fire you because, well, you never acquired the right skills, okay? And that's up to who? That's up to us, right, as a salesperson, right? Like I said, you're gonna get punched in the nose if you don't know how to trigger your prospects to want to open up and engage, okay? Now, you learn NEPQ, neuroemotional persuasion questioning, okay? That seven-level teaches, Jeremy Minor teaches, and are obviously awesome offices all over the globe.
You learn NEPQ, guess what happens? The cons start to just disappear, and you have lots of pros. So follow the pros here. High commission, high commission rate. You wanna negotiate that with a very low base. I would say negotiate no base at all once you acquire the right skills. You're gonna have lots of flexibility. They're gonna let you work anywhere you want. You're gonna learn how to sell now before you even get the job so you sell day number one. You know what to do. You're gonna help your prospects find and solve problems where they're thanking you for you, allowing them to buy from you to get where they want to get their problem solved That's a good feeling, and you're gonna get recognized in your industry by your peers, by your office, by your management teams, by your boss, by the ownership of the company.
I can assure you they fly you all over. They took me to Hawaii. They took me to Puerto Rico. They took me everywhere, gave me the big corner office. Like I said, I had a bigger office than the freaking CEO and owner of the company at one point in that business. That's one of the four industries I sold in.
